description: This scanning electron microscopy (SEM) secondary-electron image, acquired at 20 kV and ×5,000 magnification with a 5 μm scale bar, displays the microstructure of the 12Al-20Mn-1C Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steel after tempering at 800 °C for 30 min. The field of view reveals a fully decomposed austenitic structure dominated by wide, thick lath-like κ-carbide lamellae produced via cellular transformation, appearing as alternating light and dark bands. The upper-left region exhibits a finer, mesh-like contrast suggestive of spinodally decomposed austenite or a differently oriented cellular colony. Compared with the 9Al-10Mn-1C alloy treated under the same conditions, the κ-carbide strips are markedly broader and thicker at 12 wt% Al, resulting in larger inter-lamellar spacing. The caption and citation text report that these coarse lamellae yield a robust hardening response (404 HV) despite the wide spacing, and that Mn-γ stabilization is balanced by the Al–C driving force for κ-carbide formation. This figure thus provides direct morphological evidence linking high-Al chemistry to thickened cellular lamellae during full austenitic decomposition at 800 °C.
